1: ʾēlêkā YHWH napšî ʾeśśāʾ (ʾēlêḵā YHWH napšî ʾeśśāʾ): 'To you, O LORD, I lift up my soul.' Psalm 25 is an acrostic psalm — each verse begins with the successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet. The opening gesture — lifting the soul (nepeš) to YHWH — is an act of total self-offering: the self (nepeš = life-breath, will, appetite) directed entirely toward God. The preposition ʾēlêkā ('toward you') and the perfect tense of surrender set the psalm's posture: not merely asking but orienting the entire self toward the divine.
2: ʾĕlōhay bĕkā bāṭaḥtî ʾal-ʾēbôšâ (ʾĕlōhay bĕḵā ḇāṭaḥtî ʾal-ʾēḇôšâ): 'O my God, in you I trust; let me not be put to shame.' The pairing bāṭaḥtî ('I trust') with ʾal-ʾēbôšâ ('let me not be put to shame') shows that shame is the ultimate risk of trust: if YHWH does not respond, the psalmist's public reliance is exposed as futile. The phrase ʾal-yaʿalĕṣû ʾōyĕbay lî, 'let not my enemies exult over me,' is the social dimension — the enemy's victory would be the psalmist's humiliation. Trust in God is a public stake that requires God to act in order to vindicate both the psalmist and himself.
3: gam kol-qōwêkā lōʾ yēbōšû (ḡam ḵol-qōwêḵā lōʾ yēḇōšû): 'Indeed, none who wait for you shall be put to shame.' The qāwâ ('to wait, hope with taut expectation') group — all those who wait on YHWH — are collectively protected from shame. The contrast: yēbōšû habbôgĕdîm rêqām, 'those who are wantonly treacherous shall be put to shame.' The word rêqām ('empty-handed, in vain') characterizes the treacherous: they pursue betrayal but achieve nothing lasting. Shame is redirected from the trusting to the treacherous.
4: dĕrāḵêkā YHWH hôdîʿēnî ʾōrĕḥôtêkā lammĕdēnî (dĕrāḵêḵā YHWH hôḏîʿēnî ʾōrĕḥôṯêḵā lammĕḏēnî): 'Make me to know your ways, O LORD; teach me your paths.' The two pedagogical imperatives — hôdîʿēnî ('make me know') and lammĕdēnî ('teach me') — are requests for covenantal guidance. The pair dĕrāḵêkā ('your ways') and ʾōrĕḥôtêkā ('your paths') — both plural — suggest multiple dimensions of the divine will, not a single rigid rule. The request is not for information but for formation: to be shaped into a person who walks in YHWH's ways habitually.
5: haddirēkēnî bĕʾamittĕkā wĕlammĕdēnî (haḏḏirēḵēnî ḇĕʾămiṯṯĕḵā wĕlammĕḏēnî): 'Lead me in your truth and teach me.' The hiphil haddirēkēnî ('lead, guide') from dārak ('to tread a path') asks for active divine direction, not passive instruction. The prepositional phrase bĕʾamittĕkā ('in your truth/faithfulness') uses ʾĕmet ('truth, reliability') as the medium of guidance: YHWH's truth is not merely propositional but the way on which one walks. The confession: kî ʾattâ ʾĕlōhê yišʿî, 'for you are the God of my salvation' — grounds the request in covenant identity. The phrase ʾôtĕkā qiwwîtî kol-hayyôm ('you I wait for all the day') sustains the ʿal-qōwêkā of v.3.
6: zĕkōr-raḥămêkā YHWH waḥăsādeykā kî mēʿôlām hēmmâ (zĕḵor-raḥămêḵā YHWH waḥăsāḏeḵā kî mēʿôlām hēmmâ): 'Remember your mercy, O LORD, and your steadfast love, for they have been from of old.' The imperative zĕkōr ('remember!') is the covenantal appeal: YHWH's raḥămîm ('mercies, compassion' — from reḥem/'womb,' the deep visceral tenderness of a mother) and ḥăsādîm ('acts of steadfast love') have a history — mēʿôlām hēmmâ, 'they have been from of old.' The prayer is not asking for a novel attribute but for the continuation of YHWH's established pattern of compassion. Memory becomes the engine of covenant faithfulness.
7: ḥaṭṭōʾt nĕʿûray ûpĕšāʿay ʾal-tizkor (ḥaṭṭōʾṯ nĕʿûray ûp̄ĕšāʿay ʾal-tizkor): 'Remember not the sins of my youth or my transgressions.' The prayer inverts v.6: the psalmist asks God to remember (ḥăsādîm) and to forget (ḥaṭṭōʾt). The pairing ḥaṭṭōʾt ('sins, failures to hit the mark') and pĕšāʿîm ('transgressions, deliberate rebellions') covers the full range of moral failure. The phrase nĕʿûray ('of my youth') acknowledges that the young have always been vulnerable to folly — the past is real but need not define the future. The appeal: kĕḥasdĕkā zĕkār-lî ʾattâ lĕmaʿan ṭûbĕkā YHWH, 'according to your steadfast love remember me, for your goodness' sake, O LORD' — the ground of forgiveness is divine character (ḥesed, ṭôb), not human merit.
8: ṭôb wĕyāšār YHWH ʿal-kēn yôreh ḥaṭṭāʾîm baddārek (ṭôḇ wĕyāšār YHWH ʿal-kēn yôreh ḥaṭṭāʾîm baḏḏāreḵ): 'Good and upright is the LORD; therefore he instructs sinners in the way.' The declaration ṭôb wĕyāšār ('good and upright') describes YHWH's character. The logical connector ʿal-kēn ('therefore, on account of this') makes the instruction of sinners a consequence of divine character, not human worthiness: YHWH teaches sinners precisely because he is good. The noun ḥaṭṭāʾîm ('sinners') — the same people whose sins are addressed in v.7 — are not excluded from divine guidance; they are its target. This is grace-as-pedagogy.
9: yanhēl ʿănāwîm bammišpāṭ wîlammēd ʿănāwîm darkô (yanĕhēl ʿănāwîm bammišpāṭ wîlammēḏ ʿănāwîm darkô): 'He leads the humble in what is right, and teaches the humble his way.' The key word ʿănāwîm ('humble, meek, afflicted') — those who are bent down, whether by external oppression or by internal posture — are the specific recipients of divine guidance. The noun mišpāṭ ('justice, right') as the medium of leading means YHWH guides through his established order of justice, not arbitrary preference. Jesus's beatitude 'blessed are the meek' (Matt 5:5) echoes the Psalter's consistent elevation of the ʿānāw as the one who inherits the earth (Ps 37:11).
10: kol-ʾōrĕḥôt YHWH ḥesed wĕʾĕmet lĕnōṣĕrê bĕrîtô wĕʿēdōtāyw (ḵol-ʾōrĕḥôṯ YHWH ḥeseḏ wĕʾĕmeṯ lĕnōṣĕrê ḇĕrîṯô wĕʿēḏōṯāyw): 'All the paths of the LORD are steadfast love and faithfulness, for those who keep his covenant and his testimonies.' The comprehensive claim kol-ʾōrĕḥôt ('all the paths') eliminates exceptions: every way YHWH leads is characterized by ḥesed wĕʾĕmet ('steadfast love and faithfulness'). The qualifier lĕnōṣĕrê bĕrîtô wĕʿēdōtāyw, 'for those who keep his covenant and his testimonies,' identifies the covenant community as the context in which this promise operates — not a universal guarantee but a covenant-shaped assurance.
11: lĕmaʿan šimkā YHWH wĕsālaḥtā laʿăwōnî kî rāb hûʾ (lĕmaʿan šimkā YHWH wĕsālaḥtā laʿăwōnî kî rāḇ hûʾ): 'For your name's sake, O LORD, pardon my guilt, for it is great.' The appeal lĕmaʿan šimkā ('for your name's sake') grounds the request in divine honor rather than human claim: the psalmist does not argue his worthiness but YHWH's reputation. The strikingly honest phrase kî rāb hûʾ, 'for it is great' — the guilt's greatness is the very reason for the request. The logic inverts: normally great guilt would disqualify; here it intensifies the appeal. Only a God of extraordinary grace can handle great guilt — and YHWH is that God.
12: mî-zeh hāʾîš yĕrēʾ YHWH yôrennû bĕdārek yibḥār (mî-zeh hāʾîš yĕrēʾ YHWH yôrennû bĕḏāreḵ yiḇḥār): 'Who is the man who fears the LORD? Him will he instruct in the way that he should choose.' The rhetorical question mî-zeh hāʾîš, 'who is the man?' recalls Ps 24:3-4 — the entrance liturgy question about qualification. Here the qualifier is yĕrēʾ YHWH ('fearing the LORD') — the foundational orientation from which all wisdom flows (Prov 1:7). The result: yôrennû bĕdārek yibḥār, 'he will instruct him in the way he should choose.' YHWH teaches the God-fearer how to make life's choices — wisdom as the fruit of reverence.
13: napšô bĕṭôb tālîn wĕzarʿô yîraš ʾāreṣ (napšô bĕṭôḇ tālîn wĕzarʿô yîraš ʾāreṣ): 'His soul shall abide in well-being, and his offspring shall inherit the land.' The promise napšô bĕṭôb tālîn, 'his soul shall lodge in prosperity' — using lûn ('to lodge overnight, rest') suggests the well-being is settled and abiding, not temporary. The generational extension wĕzarʿô yîraš ʾāreṣ, 'and his offspring shall inherit the land,' draws on the covenantal promise of the land (Gen 12:7; Ps 37:11). The blessing of the God-fearing extends beyond the individual to the next generation. Matt 5:5's 'they shall inherit the earth' universalizes the land promise.
14: sôd YHWH lîrēʾāyw ûbĕrîtô lĕhôdîʿām (sôḏ YHWH lîrēʾāyw ûḇĕrîṯô lĕhôḏîʿām): 'The friendship of the LORD is for those who fear him, and he makes known to them his covenant.' The noun sôd ('council, intimate circle, secret counsel') — used of the divine council (Jer 23:18; Job 15:8) — here describes the intimate fellowship YHWH offers to the God-fearing: not merely information about the covenant but inclusion in YHWH's own inner circle. The phrase ûbĕrîtô lĕhôdîʿām, 'and he makes his covenant known to them,' is the content of the sôd: covenant-knowledge as the shared secret of divine friendship.
15: ʿênay tāmîd ʾel-YHWH kî hûʾ yôṣî mĕrešet raglāy (ʿênay tāmîd ʾel-YHWH kî hûʾ yôṣî mĕrešeṯ raglāy): 'My eyes are ever toward the LORD, for he will pluck my feet out of the net.' The phrase ʿênay tāmîd ʾel-YHWH, 'my eyes always toward the LORD,' describes the posture of constant orientation — not periodic turning but sustained gaze. The ground kî hûʾ yôṣî mĕrešet raglāy, 'for he will pluck my feet out of the net,' uses yāṣāʾ (hiphil, 'to bring out, pluck out') for rescue from the reŝet ('net, snare') — the traps set by enemies (cf. Ps 10:9; 22:20). Trust is gaze; gaze enables rescue.
16: pĕnēh-ʾēlay wĕḥonnēnî kî-yāḥîd wĕʿānî ʾānî (p̄ĕnēh-ʾēlay wĕḥonnēnî kî-yāḥîd wĕʿānî ʾānî): 'Turn to me and be gracious to me, for I am lonely and afflicted.' The double self-description yāḥîd ('alone, solitary') and ʿānî ('afflicted, poor') identifies the psalmist's condition: isolated and beaten down. The imperative pĕnēh ('turn toward me!') — literally the divine face turning — requests the personal attention of YHWH. The word ḥonnēnî ('be gracious to me!') invokes the ḥānan/ḥesed cluster of divine favor. Loneliness before God is the psalm's deepest pain: not merely suffering but suffering in perceived isolation from the divine.
17: ṣārôt lĕbābî hirḥîbû mimmeṣûqôtay hôṣîʾēnî (ṣārôt lĕḇāḇî hirḥîḇû mimmeṣûqôṯay hôṣîʾēnî): 'The troubles of my heart are enlarged; bring me out of my distresses.' The idiom ṣārôt lĕbābî hirḥîbû ('the troubles of my heart have enlarged') describes how distress expands to fill the interior — anxieties that grow until they dominate the entire inner landscape. The imperative hôṣîʾēnî ('bring me out!') from yāṣāʾ ('to go out') is the rescue term — the same verb used in Exod 13:3 for the Exodus. The psalmist prays for a personal exodus from his meṣûqôt ('straits, tight places').
18: rĕʾēh ʿonyî waʿămālî wĕśāʾ lĕkol-ḥaṭṭōʾtāy (rĕʾēh ʿonyî waʿămālî wĕśāʾ lĕḵol-ḥaṭṭōʾṯāy): 'Consider my affliction and my trouble, and forgive all my sins.' The imperative rĕʾēh ('see, consider') requests divine attention to the external (ʿonî, 'affliction') and internal (ʿāmāl, 'toil, trouble') dimensions of suffering. The addition wĕśāʾ lĕkol-ḥaṭṭōʾtāy, 'and lift off all my sins,' connects suffering to sin — not necessarily causally but covenantally: the psalmist acknowledges that complete deliverance requires both external rescue and internal forgiveness. The verb nāśāʾ ('to lift up, carry away') as the verb for forgiveness captures the removal of the burden (cf. Lev 16:22).
19: rĕʾēh ʾôyĕbay kî rabbû wĕśinʾat ḥāmās śĕnēʾûnî (rĕʾēh ʾôyĕbay kî rabbû wĕśinʾaṯ ḥāmās śĕnēʾûnî): 'Consider how many are my enemies, and with what violent hatred they hate me.' The resumptive rĕʾēh ('consider/see') repeats v.18's opening: look at both the sin (internal) and the enemies (external). The phrase śinʾat ḥāmās śĕnēʾûnî ('with hatred of violence they hate me') is a cognate accusative intensifier — the hating is characterized by ḥāmās ('violence, destructive force'). The enemies' hatred is not merely emotional but operational: they intend violence.
20: šomrâ napšî wĕhaṣṣîlēnî ʾal-ʾēbôš kî ḥāsîtî bāk (šomrâ napšî wĕhaṣṣîlēnî ʾal-ʾēḇôš kî ḥāsîṯî ḇāḵ): 'Keep my soul and deliver me; let me not be put to shame, for I take refuge in you.' The paired imperatives šomrâ ('guard!') and haṣṣîlēnî ('deliver!') bracket the prayer: constant protection and active rescue. The shame-avoidance ʾal-ʾēbôš recalls v.2 — the psalm is framed by the fear of shame. The ground ḥāsîtî bāk ('I have taken refuge in you') is the basis: since the psalmist has publicly committed to YHWH as refuge, YHWH's honor is bound up with the outcome.
21: tōm wĕyōšer yiṣṣĕrûnî kî qiwwîtîkā (ṯōm wĕyōšer yiṣṣĕrûnî kî qiwwîtîkā): 'May integrity and uprightness preserve me, for I wait for you.' The nouns tōm ('completeness, integrity') and yōšer ('uprightness, straightness') function as guardians — the psalmist's own moral character as protective companions. The reason: kî qiwwîtîkā, 'for I wait for you' — covenant faithfulness expressed as patient, taut expectation. The verse restates the psalm's posture: integrity-shaped waiting as the practical form of covenant trust.
22: pĕdēh ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-yiśrāʾēl mikkōl ṣārôtāyw (p̄ĕḏēh ʾĕlōhîm ʾeṯ-yiśrāʾēl miḵḵōl ṣārôṯāyw): 'Redeem Israel, O God, out of all his troubles.' The closing verse breaks the acrostic (it is a pê verse appended after the ṣādê verse) and shifts from individual to national concern. The verb pādâ ('redeem, ransom') — one of the key exodus/salvation verbs — applied to Israel as a whole suggests that the individual psalmist's experience of needing rescue mirrors Israel's corporate situation. The move from personal prayer to corporate intercession is characteristic of psalmic theology: the individual's faith is never separable from the community's hope.
